title: Postmortem - Incident #8842: ECS Tasks Repeatedly Drained Due to Health-Check Misconfiguration
content:
Postmortem - Incident #8842: ECS Tasks Repeatedly Drained Due to Health-Check Misconfiguration

### Incident Summary
The `ecs-fargate-01` service entered a drain-and-replace loop shortly after a configuration rollout. Tasks launched successfully but were marked unhealthy before startup completed, causing continuous churn and loss of worker throughput.

### Root Cause
A target group health-check path and grace period were misaligned with the application startup sequence. The container needed additional warm-up time and did not expose the expected health path early enough, so ECS replaced healthy-but-not-ready tasks.

### Contributing Factors
- Startup secret fetch and dependency warm-up added 20-30 seconds to boot time.
- Desired count was left high during the failure, amplifying task churn and log noise.
- Service events were initially misread as application crashes rather than readiness failures.

### Resolution
- Lowered desired count to stabilize the environment during investigation.
- Reviewed target health, ECS service events, and stopped task history.
- Increased health-check grace period and corrected the health endpoint path.
- Redeployed one task definition revision and confirmed stable healthy targets before scaling back up.

### Lessons Learned
- Repeated ECS draining often indicates readiness or health-check mismatch, not always a code crash.
- Grace periods must account for dependency initialization and secret retrieval.
- The safest recovery pattern is reduce churn, validate one healthy task, then restore capacity gradually.
